terms.Product_Market_Fit: Being in a good market with a product that consistently solves a meaningful problem for a clearly defined group, where users keep coming back, tell others, and pay in a way supporting sustainable business. Achieved when retaining 40%+ of activated users month-over-month (Sean Ellis benchmark). Takes roughly 2 years; 80% of products never get there.
terms.Lean_Startup: Methodology based on Build-Measure-Learn cycle: develop MVPs quickly, measure performance, collect feedback, learn, iterate. Tests problem-solution fit through customer development interviews before building. Originated by Eric Ries. Core principle: fail fast, learn faster.
terms.Customer_Development: Steve Blank's methodology for validating startup hypotheses before building. Four steps: Customer Discovery (validate problem exists), Customer Validation (validate solution), Customer Creation (scale), Company Building (transition to execution). Separates problem from solution in early interviews.
terms.MVP: Minimum Viable Product — simplest version of product that allows team to collect maximum amount of validated learning about customers with least effort. Not about minimum features — about maximum learning. Purpose: test core hypothesis before full build.
terms.Pivot: Structured course correction designed to test a new fundamental hypothesis about product, strategy, and growth engine. Data-driven decision. Types: Zoom-in, Zoom-out, Customer Segment, Customer Need, Platform, Business Architecture, Value Capture, Engine of Growth, Channel, Technology.
terms.Traction: Evidence that a startup is gaining market momentum. Measured via Pirate Metrics (AARRR): Acquisition (how customers find you), Activation (first great experience), Retention (keep coming back), Referral (tell others), Revenue (paying). PMF signal: strong retention + organic referral.
terms.Pirate_Metrics_AARRR: Framework by Dave McClure: Acquisition (traffic/sign-ups), Activation (first value experience), Retention (repeat engagement), Referral (NPS, viral coefficient), Revenue (conversion to paying). Used to identify which part of funnel is broken.
terms.Problem_Solution_Fit: Stage before product-market fit. Validation that your proposed solution solves a real, painful problem for specific customers. Validated through customer discovery interviews before any product is built. Must precede MVP development.

terms.TAM: Total Addressable Market — total revenue opportunity if you sold to 100% of the market. Panoramic view of all possible demand. Venture-scale typically requires TAM > $1B. Calculated via top-down (Gartner/IDC reports) or bottom-up (customers × price).
terms.SAM: Serviceable Available Market — realistic portion of TAM your business can practically serve at this stage based on current resources, team, capabilities, and strategy. Geographic, vertical, or channel constrained slice of TAM.
terms.SOM: Serviceable Obtainable Market — what you'll realistically win against competitors in 3-5 years. Your defensible share of SAM. Should be backed by bottom-up customer analysis. This is what goes in your financial model Year 3-5 projection.
terms.Bottoms_Up: Market sizing methodology building from ground up: number of target customers × price per customer. Forces precise customer definition. Preferred by investors — grounded in real assumptions. More credible than top-down for SOM calculation.
terms.Top_Down: Market sizing methodology starting from broad industry level and narrowing: industry TAM from research firms → filter for your segment. Fast and useful for TAM context. Typically from Gartner, IDC, IBISWorld, Statista reports. Combine with bottom-up for credibility.

terms.CAC: Customer Acquisition Cost = Total Sales & Marketing Expenses / New Customers Acquired in Period. Include salaries, tools, ad spend, events, content. Common mistake: under-counting costs. Blended CAC includes all channels; paid CAC is channel-specific.
terms.LTV: Lifetime Value = (Average Revenue Per Account × Gross Margin) / Churn Rate. Estimates total revenue a customer generates over entire relationship. For SaaS: LTV = ARPU × Gross Margin % / Monthly Churn Rate.
terms.LTV_CAC: LTV:CAC ratio benchmark: 3:1 is minimum for healthy unit economics; 3:1-5:1 is sweet spot for growth-stage; >5:1 suggests underinvestment in growth. Series A: 3x. Series B/C: 5x+. Below 1:1 means company destroys value with each customer.
terms.Payback_Period: CAC Payback Period = CAC / (Monthly Revenue per Customer × Gross Margin). Measures time to recover customer acquisition cost. Benchmarks: <12 months = excellent (B2B SaaS); 12-18 months = good; >24 months = concerning. Median: 6.8 months overall, 8.6 months B2B SaaS.
terms.Gross_Margin: = (Revenue - COGS) / Revenue. For SaaS: 70-80%+ is target. Below 70% suggests structural issue (too much cloud infra cost, professional services heavy, marketplace with high payment fees). Gross margin expansion is key value driver.
terms.Churn_Rate: Percentage of customers/revenue lost in a period. Monthly churn of 1-2% = good SaaS. Annual = 10-15% acceptable, <5% excellent. Net Revenue Retention (NRR/NDR) > 100% means expansion revenue exceeds churn — best metric for SaaS health.
terms.ARR: Annual Recurring Revenue = MRR × 12. Foundation metric for SaaS valuation. ARR multiples (EV/ARR) range: 5-15x for high-growth SaaS (2024 market). ARR growth rate of 3x+ year-over-year = T2D3 benchmark for Series A/B SaaS.
terms.MRR: Monthly Recurring Revenue — predictable, recurring revenue from subscriptions. Components: New MRR (new customers) + Expansion MRR (upsells) - Churned MRR (cancellations) - Contraction MRR (downgrades) = Net New MRR.
terms.Burn_Rate: Net cash consumed per month. Gross burn = total monthly expenses. Net burn = gross burn - revenue. Key metric for runway calculation. VCs want to see burn multiple (net burn / net new ARR) below 1.5x for efficient growth.
terms.Runway: Months of cash remaining = Cash Balance / Monthly Net Burn. VCs typically expect startups to raise with 18-24 months runway. Rule of thumb: raise before you have < 6 months runway. Extend runway by cutting burn or generating revenue faster.
